stem: A hospital uses a PSA blood test to screen for prostate cancer. The test has a sensitivity of 89% and a specificity of 94%. The prevalence of prostate cancer in the patient population is estimated to be between 2.2% and 4.5%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has prostate cancer? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(prostate cancer | positive test) ranges from 0.25 to 0.411 as prevalence varies from 2.2% to 4.5%. Point estimate at 3.4% prevalence: 0.343.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.06.
The confidence interval [0.25, 0.411] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (89%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
